speaks differently than does a two column page. It's more serious journalism. It's what we would expect out of a trade journal or Newsweek or Time. Two columns is maybe more what we see in newsletters we get from people. It's a little more casual. So all of that I think is, you know, important. And I hate it when clients change my type style. Now, this may or may not, this trivia, this may or may not interest you. This will make it worse for you, by the way. But I pretty much do the type styles as I write the copy. So I'll only go through four paragraphs and then I've got to make it look like the way I want to make it to look before I go any further. There's like a choice of 900 type styles. Obviously there's some you obviously don't want to have. There's some you don't want to have. There's some you rely on more than others. It's why I'm a mat guy. I like to type more than anything else. I like to type faces better than I do anything else. But so I'm working with a pallet of about 50. But then there's size issues. And then there's all the cosmetic issues of do you bold face it, not bold face it, italicize it, not italicize it, underline it, not underline it, shadow it, not shadow it. You know, etc. And I will often spend, oh I don't know, 15 minutes writing a page and 15 minutes screwing around with the way it looks. Dan, you don't write in the templates. You just write the word and it goes through all the other stuff. I use the thing as a typewriter and an appearance machine. Yeah, I don't. Yeah, Karl puts it all into the... I have a question, I want to get both you and Bill to comment on it. Two questions. One is in terms of your promotions and themes, you guys obviously get together, you've got, I would have managed a majority of your promotions plan for O8. You know what I'm doing? He does. One of us does. Yeah, one of us does. So you've got major promotions already set on the calendar, so you know when you're going to start pushing those in the newsletter element. How much of that though, in terms of the themes though, and some of the other elements that go in here are pretty planned ahead of time. I'm sure it's different, but just to get your feedback and comment on it, and how much of it is, well this is what I've got in the file this month. Well these two things, when I get down and sit down and write. Well, so you know, what I do is I rate them in order importance. So like Dan said, the two most important promotions are the Info Summit and the Supercomps. So those are like set in stone, and we know precisely when they're going to be launched, when they're going to be a heavy marketing door, and that like for instance, in beginning a week from Monday, you'll start seeing a barrage for the Info Summit. So that takes precedence, that's the first thing that happens. And then we take a look at our second tier promotions. So things such as every year there's, or sometimes even twice a year there's an upgrade to Gold Plus campaign going on. There's a, you can't get the, I'm marking to the affluent newsletter pitch that goes on. Those that sort of be second tier promotions. And there's a whole bunch of those. Those get sort of mapped out to, these are now months that are kind of clearer than other months. So this area gets, happens there. And there's third tier promotions. So like for example, I handed out a sales letter for a new product and it's great, great. And that will be inserted in wherever there's an opening. Yeah, sometimes I would know about those until the month I'm doing it. They'll, I'll get a fax that says this month, this sales letter is going in with the newsletter. This month we're giving away a mouse pad to everybody for Christmas. This month we're doing that. You make sure, see if you can integrate it into the newsletter. So my fight with the editors is not just about copy, it's about appearance. And we fight a lot. So like a long time ago I won the subhead battle. And so now they pretty much leave me alone with that. But that was a fight. Too many subheads, why are they in bold face, why is this one in full caps, yada, yada, yada. Well in terms of the newsletters, how do you guys do the scheduling, I mean, planning wise? Like you know, is the November newsletter done 30 days out of time, 2 days out of time, 60 days, and the handoff of handling the filming sites, the train staying on the tracks, with everything going on. There's a lot of moving parts. It's really the answer. So I mean, once Dan writes the newsletter and then Carla gets into format, she gets my article, she gets Yannick's article. Then it goes back to Dan for him to look at, then it comes, then it gets back to Carla, forwarded to the printer. In my office there's somebody that's coordinating that whole piece too, because there's a lot of things that have to get, there's a lot of moving parts that a lot of things could go wrong. But like getting back to your point as far as the newsletter gets done 30 days before it goes out, that's not true because Dan wants to justifiably keep it as current as possible with what's going on time wise. So there's a lot of real tight time schedules that are happening. Dan's letter is probably from my finishing hours to the time it's going to the printer, we're dealing with about seven or eight days, I would think, in most cases. Now that, by the way, you could do it a month ahead of time, and you might not sacrifice a lot. So some of that is also accommodating my eccentricities. And the thing, I mean, I'm sure he would rather not, but the thing sort of has to accommodate some of my eccentricities, because I'm the guy doing a, you know, and so one of my eccentricities is I don't like to work on something until it's like the next thing that needs to be done. So like they would like to have had my handouts for next weekend seminar last week, so they could have got them done to give to everybody, and that ain't gonna happen, because I did this. Now, next week I'll do that thing, and that's what works best for me. So you're doing some lentil with something? No, no, no, I got 20 things going on simultaneously, but there are all 20 things that need to be done right now. That needs to be done a week from now. Smart as. Yeah, smart as. So some of it is accommodating your personal, how do you work best, right, and then sort of building the system to facilitate how you work best.
